guides.guideDescription: Guidelines developed by the European Association of Science Editors (EASE) to improve clarity, completeness, and international readability of scientific manuscripts written in English.

analysis.item_question: Is the title unambiguous, specific, and reflective of the article content?
analysis.item_question_background: Vague or ambiguous titles impair discoverability and may mislead readers about the study scope, increasing citation distortion and misclassification in indexing databases.
analysis.comment: The title is specific and reflects the manuscript’s focus on soy protein substitution for animal meat proteins and the resulting global phosphorus reduction and recirculation implications.
quotes: A s oy protein substitute for animal meat proteins can provide global phosphorus reduction and recirculation opportunities

analysis.item_question: If relevant, does the title include study period, location, organism scientific name, or experimental design?
analysis.item_question_background: Omitting contextual identifiers such as location, species, or design reduces reproducibility and prevents accurate interpretation of study generalizability.
analysis.comment: The title includes useful scope context through the global framing, but it does not include a study period, experimental design, or a specific organism scientific name.
quotes: A s oy protein substitute for animal meat proteins can provide global phosphorus reduction and recirculation opportunities

analysis.item_question: If human subjects of only one sex were included, is this specified in the title?
analysis.item_question_background: Failure to disclose sex-restricted sampling obscures biological applicability and may contribute to sex-based bias in interpretation and downstream clinical translation.
analysis.comment: This item is not evidently relevant because the manuscript is not a single-sex human-subject study, and I could not locate supporting evidence indicating a one-sex-only population in the manuscript content provided for this request.

analysis.item_question: Are all authors listed with affiliations, present correspondence address, and email addresses?
analysis.item_question_background: Incomplete author metadata prevents accountability, inhibits correspondence, and complicates institutional attribution and research integrity verification.
analysis.comment: All authors are listed with affiliations, and a corresponding author postal address and email are provided. However, email addresses are not provided for all authors; only the corresponding author’s email is visible.

analysis.item_question: Does the abstract contain background, objectives, methods, results, and conclusions?
analysis.item_question_background: Structured abstracts reduce selective outcome emphasis and improve indexing accuracy by ensuring balanced representation of study rationale, execution, and findings.
analysis.comment: The abstract includes background/rationale, methods, results, and a concluding implication, but it does not clearly state an explicit objective or aim within the abstract itself.
quotes: Animal meat protein consumption is typically associated with greater environmental impacts than plant - based proteins . While most environmental impact assessments comparing animal and plant - based proteins focus on water usage and carbon emissions, phosphorus (P) use is a key parameter for a sustainable food system. C ountry - specific animal and crop parameters were leveraged to evaluate the P footprint of current animal meat protein consumption and potential benefits from substitution using soy - based protein.

The global average P footprint for animal meat protein consumption when considering synthetic and manure P fertilizers was an estimated 2.6 kg P y - 1 per capita in 2019 . A complete transition to soy protein concentrate (SPC) from animal meat protein was estimated to decrease total P fertilizer applied to animal feed products by 81 % , or an estimated 8.3 million metric tons of P per year and 33% of the total estimated 2019 P fertilizer utilization . Additionally, t he recovery of P during SPC processing could generate sufficient renewable P fertilizer to replace an estimated 17% of total P utilized in global soybean production. The reduction and efficient reuse of P from a transition to SPC as a primary protein source can generate greater security and resiliency in global food systems.

analysis.item_question: Does the abstract avoid references to tables or figures?
analysis.item_question_background: Abstracts are often indexed independently; referencing tables or figures creates inaccessible claims when separated from the full manuscript.
analysis.comment: The abstract avoids references to tables or figures.
analysis.quote_verification_note: This item is satisfied because the manuscript avoids prohibited wording, so there may be no supporting quote.

analysis.item_question: Does the abstract avoid literature references unless absolutely necessary and fully detailed?
analysis.item_question_background: Citations in abstracts can mislead indexing systems and create incomplete bibliographic records when abstracts are distributed without reference lists.
analysis.comment: The abstract avoids literature references/citations.
analysis.quote_verification_note: This item is satisfied because the manuscript avoids prohibited wording, so there may be no supporting quote.

analysis.item_question: Is all information presented in the abstract also reported in the main body?
analysis.item_question_background: Discrepancies between abstract and body may constitute selective reporting and distort reader interpretation or meta-analytic extraction.
analysis.comment: The key quantitative and interpretive statements made in the abstract are also reported in the main body.
quotes: The 2019 global average P footprint of fertilizer use for animal meat consumption was an estimated 2.6 kg P per capita.

Substitution of animal - based proteins with SPC could reduce non - roughage feed P utilization by as much as 81 % , or 33% of all P inputs in 2019 ... Recovery of r P during SPC processing through chemical separation and reuse as a renewable fertilizer could replace an estimated 17% of P fertilizer (0.34 Mt rP per year) used in soybean production globally.

analysis.item_question: Do keywords include relevant scientific terms not already in the title?
analysis.item_question_background: Redundant keywords reduce indexing breadth and may limit discoverability across thematic databases.
analysis.comment: The keyword list is present, but two of the three keywords repeat terms already used in the title ('Soy protein' and 'phosphorus'), limiting added indexing breadth. One keyword adds a concept not explicit in the title ('phosphorus recovery').

A s oy protein substitute for animal meat proteins can provide global phosphorus reduction and recirculation opportunities

analysis.item_question: Are the keywords specific rather than general?
analysis.item_question_background: Overly broad keywords dilute search precision and impair targeted retrieval in bibliographic databases.
analysis.comment: The keywords are reasonably specific scientific terms rather than overly broad general descriptors.
quotes: Keywords: Soy protein, phosphorus recovery, phosphorus footprint

analysis.item_question: Are broader interdisciplinary terms included when appropriate?
analysis.item_question_background: Failure to include cross-disciplinary indexing terms can isolate research from adjacent fields where findings may have translational relevance.
analysis.comment: The study spans food systems, environmental sustainability, and nutrient recovery across countries, but the keywords focus narrowly on soy protein and phosphorus concepts and do not clearly include a broader interdisciplinary indexing term such as food systems, sustainability, circular economy, or global agriculture.
quotes: Keywords: Soy protein, phosphorus recovery, phosphorus footprint
Synopsis: This study analyzes the phosphorus benefits of substitution of animal meat protein with soy protein across 177 countries.

guides.guideDescription: Guidance developed by the European Association of Science Editors (EASE) to improve clarity, transparency, ethical compliance, and research integrity in scientific manuscripts structured according to the IMRaD format.

analysis.item_question: Does the Introduction clearly explain why the study was needed?
analysis.item_question_background: Without a clearly stated rationale, readers cannot assess the scientific gap being addressed, which undermines the justification for the study and weakens its contribution to the field.
analysis.comment: The Introduction clearly explains the study need by establishing the importance of phosphorus for food security, the higher phosphorus footprint of animal-based proteins, and the lack of quantified global and country-level phosphorus implications of replacing animal protein with soy protein concentrate.
quotes: While land use, water use, and greenhouse gas benefits of dietary plant - based protein alternatives have been generally quantified, 15 the global and country - level P implications of replacing animal protein with SPC remain poorly defined .

Moreover, because P can be recovered as a renewable P (rP) fertilizer from wastewater generated during SPC processing , 23 the circularity of P afforded by substitution to soybean protein has not been accounted for in previous studies, underestimating the potential reduction in P footprint.

analysis.item_question: Are the research objectives or specific research questions clearly stated?
analysis.item_question_background: If research objectives are vague or absent, readers cannot determine what the study intends to answer, compromising interpretability and evaluation of results.
analysis.comment: The research objectives are explicitly and clearly stated at the end of the Introduction as a study aim followed by three specific objectives.
quotes: T his study aims to assess the potential of SPC from existing soy production and the associated P benefits of substituting SPC for current animal - based protein s , including potential reduction s in diet ary P burden and increased P circularity.

The specific objectives are to : 1) estimate current P utilization and footprint s for animal meat consumption in 177 countries, based on annual synthetic and organic (i.e., manure) P fertilizer inputs , leveraging country - and region - specific parameters and trade data ; 2) quantify the country - level potential to substitute SPC , produced from existing soybean meal, for animal meat protein (i.e., beef, goat/mutton, pig, and poultry) and the associated P reduction benefits ; and 3) evaluate the circularity potential of rP from SPC processing compared to P recycled via animal manure as a P fertilizer source .

analysis.item_question: Does the Introduction move from general background to the specific research question?
analysis.item_question_background: A poorly structured introduction can obscure the logical development of the research problem, making it difficult to follow how the specific question emerges from broader context.
analysis.comment: The Introduction follows a logical progression from broad background on phosphorus sustainability, to dietary protein source impacts, to soy protein concentrate as a candidate substitute, then to the unresolved gap and the study objective.
quotes: Phosphorus (P) is an essential crop nutrient critical to global food security.
To fully evaluate the P reduction and reuse benefits of SPC substitution, country - level P footprints and circularity must be individually determined.

analysis.item_question: If applicable, is the tested hypothesis explicitly formulated?
analysis.item_question_background: Failure to state a hypothesis in hypothesis-driven research obscures the analytical framework and limits the reader’s ability to evaluate whether conclusions align with prespecified expectations.
analysis.comment: I could not locate an explicit hypothesis statement in the manuscript content provided for this request.

analysis.item_question: Does the Methods section describe in sufficient detail how the study was carried out?
analysis.item_question_background: Insufficient methodological detail prevents reproducibility and limits the ability of other researchers to verify or replicate the findings.
analysis.comment: The Methods section gives substantial procedural detail on data sources, scope, equations, parameter definitions, substitution modeling, phosphorus recirculation calculations, and uncertainty analysis, sufficient to understand and largely reproduce the study workflow.
quotes: All data sources were limited to open access databases for ease of reproducibility. T he Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) statistics database (FAOSTAT), 30 which includes country - level and trade data for primary and processed crops, plant and animal - based food use, and protein consumption was a primary data source for this analysis . Data was limited to 2019 to avoid potential effects of the COVID - 19 pandemic.

L imitation on the fraction of animal meat protein was evaluated from 0.5 to 0.0 kg animal meat protein per kg total protein. A ll animal meat protein in excess of the limit was substituted with SPC protein. The substitution process was done by reducing the amount of animal - based protein by the meat type with the highest estimated P contributions (i.e., 1 bovine meat, 2 goat/mutton meat, 3 pigmeat, and 4 poultry meat).

analysis.item_question: Are all factors that could have affected the results considered?
analysis.item_question_background: Unreported confounding or influencing factors can introduce bias and mislead interpretation of the study’s findings.
analysis.comment: The manuscript considers several important factors affecting results, including country- and region-specific parameters, trade, feed composition, manure recycling assumptions, roughage treatment, and uncertainty analysis. However, it also acknowledges simplifying assumptions and omitted factors, so not all potentially influential factors appear to be fully considered.
quotes: The primary source and geospatial scale for each parameter can be found in Table 1. In this study, PUE is estimated as the P yield (i.e., P content) over inputs (i.e., P fertilizer and manure), where Zou et al. 32 calculated PUE for each individual crop in each country.

However, the global scale of this study necessitat es several simplifying assumptions were made when generating P estimates. Though many parameters can vary among individual livestock species across varying geographical scales ( e.g. , feed conversion efficiency, feed ration composition , excretion rates ) , regional and country - based parameters were used when generating P utilization and recirculation estimates for animal meat protein

analysis.item_question: If experimental materials were obtained from biobanks, are full names and identifiers reported?
analysis.item_question_background: Failure to report biobank sources and identifiers impedes traceability of materials and may compromise reproducibility and ethical oversight.
analysis.comment: No biobank-derived experimental materials were identified, and I could not locate supporting evidence in the manuscript content provided for this request.

analysis.item_question: If citing non-English or inaccessible methods, are they fully explained?
analysis.item_question_background: Relying on inaccessible sources without detailed explanation prevents verification and limits accessibility for international readers.
analysis.comment: I could not locate evidence that the Methods rely on non-English or otherwise inaccessible methods requiring fuller explanation beyond the procedural descriptions already provided.

analysis.item_question: Does the manuscript confirm compliance with relevant ethical standards?
analysis.item_question_background: Failure to report ethical compliance raises concerns about participant protection, animal welfare, or environmental harm, potentially invalidating the study.
analysis.comment: I could not locate supporting evidence in the manuscript content provided for this request that confirms compliance with relevant ethical standards, ethics approval, or adherence to ethical guidelines.

analysis.item_question: Does the Results section present only new findings from the study?
analysis.item_question_background: Including previously published data in the Results section can confuse the originality of findings and distort the distinction between background and novel contributions.
analysis.comment: The Results and Discussion section mainly reports findings generated by this study, but it also includes comparative previously published figures and literature-based contextual data within the results narrative.
quotes: The total global P utilization of fertilizers (i.e., synthetic and manure P) to produce animal meat protein has increased an estimated 15% from 2014 to 2021 , with an estimated 19.8 million metric tons ( Mt ) P used in 2019

Comparatively, a 50% substitution of animal products with plant - based meat and milk alternatives by 2050 could nearly halt the net reduction in forest and natural land s and decrease agriculture and land use greenhouse gas emissions by 31%. 15

analysis.item_question_background: Inappropriate statistical methods can lead to incorrect conclusions and invalidate the study’s findings.
analysis.comment: The manuscript describes uncertainty and sensitivity analyses that are broadly appropriate for the modeling study, but it does not provide enough detail to fully verify correct statistical application or assumptions.

analysis.item_question: Does the Discussion avoid introducing new results?
analysis.item_question_background: Introducing new data in the Discussion blurs structural boundaries and prevents proper scrutiny of results presentation.
analysis.comment: The discussion-related sections introduce additional quantitative findings rather than only interpreting previously presented results, so the Discussion does not clearly avoid new results.
quotes: A modest decrease of 0.1 kg animal protein per kg total protein consumed could reduce the national P footprint from meat proteins by more than 50% in at least 107 countries.

In countries that eat the most meat per capita like the US , Brazil, and China, the same decrease of 0.1 kg animal protein per kg total protein consumed could reduce animal meat P footprints by an estimated 65%, 58%, and 68%, respectively.

analysis.item_question: Does the Discussion explicitly answer the research questions?
analysis.item_question_background: Failing to explicitly connect findings to the stated research questions weakens the logical coherence of the manuscript.
analysis.comment: The discussion links findings back to the stated aims by addressing the phosphorus benefits of substituting soy protein concentrate for animal protein and the implications for phosphorus sustainability and recirculation.
quotes: T his study aims to assess the potential of SPC from existing soy production and the associated P benefits of substituting SPC for current animal - based protein s , including potential reduction s in diet ary P burden and increased P circularity.

Overall, l arge P benefits were associated with plant - based SPC protein , where additional P recovery can be incentivized — providing policymakers with additional considerations when generating methods to ensure security and resiliency in food production system s nationally and globally .

analysis.item_question: Are the main results objectively compared with published data?
analysis.item_question_background: Without contextual comparison, readers cannot evaluate how the findings align with or diverge from existing evidence.
analysis.comment: The manuscript compares its results with previously published studies and interprets similarities and differences objectively.
quotes: The use of high - energy feeds in the US, which have higher feed crop PUEs than grazing, contributed to a lower P conversion factor and thus footprint . The consideration of country - specific parameters, such as PUE and trade, had a large influence on individual P footprints : the US P footprint for meat consumption was 23% lower than previous estimates that used a global average P conversion factor . 12

Comparatively, a 50% substitution of animal products with plant - based meat and milk alternatives by 2050 could nearly halt the net reduction in forest and natural land s and decrease agriculture and land use greenhouse gas emissions by 31%. 15

analysis.item_question: Are study limitations discussed?
analysis.item_question_background: Failure to disclose limitations may overstate confidence in findings and mislead readers regarding their reliability.
analysis.comment: The manuscript includes an explicit Study Limitations section that discusses simplifying assumptions, parameter aggregation, fixed-year data use, and methodological constraints.

However, the global scale of this study necessitat es several simplifying assumptions were made when generating P estimates.

analysis.item_question: Are the main findings clearly highlighted?
analysis.item_question_background: If key findings are not emphasized, the study’s primary contribution may be obscured.
analysis.comment: The Discussion clearly highlights the main findings, emphasizing the magnitude of phosphorus reductions from soy protein substitution and the recirculation benefits from SPC processing.
quotes: Human consumption of SPC from country - level soybean meal production could reduce global P utilization by an estimated 12.7 Mt P per year , or a 64% reduction .

R ecovery of r P during SPC processing through chemical separation and reuse as a renewable fertilizer could replace an estimated 17% of P fertilizer (0.34 Mt rP per year) used in soybean production globally ( Fig ure 4a )

analysis.item_question: If the study included only one sex, are implications for generalizability discussed?
analysis.item_question_background: Failing to discuss single-sex sampling limits transparency regarding external validity across sexes.
analysis.comment: Optional item. I could not locate supporting evidence in the manuscript content provided for this request that the study included only one sex or that implications for generalizability across sexes were discussed.

analysis.item_question: Are findings contrary to the authors’ viewpoint considered?

analysis.comment: The discussion considers findings and perspectives that temper the authors' main viewpoint, including limits to direct substitution, socioeconomic barriers, and cases where animal proteins may compare favorably on some phosphorus metrics.
quotes: However, SPC by itself would not be an adequate direct substitute for animal meat and would need to be combined with other plant - based components to be both palatable 15 and to have a similar nutrition profile . 60

However, some high meat consuming countries, including the US, have relatively low processing P losses and footprints from animal proteins when compared to SPC due to greater feeding efficiencies at concentrated animal feeding operations (CAFOs).

analysis.item_question: Are major conclusions and practical significance clearly emphasized?
analysis.item_question_background: Without a clear conclusion and statement of practical relevance, readers may struggle to understand the study’s real-world or theoretical impact.
analysis.comment: The manuscript clearly emphasizes the main conclusions and their practical significance in the closing outlook, highlighting phosphorus benefits, policy relevance, and implications for food-system resilience.
quotes: Overall, l arge P benefits were associated with plant - based SPC protein , where additional P recovery can be incentivized — providing policymakers with additional considerations when generating methods to ensure security and resiliency in food production system s nationally and globally .
A modest decrease of 0.1 kg animal protein per kg total protein consumed could reduce the national P footprint from meat proteins by more than 50% in at least 107 countries.

guides.guideDescription: SAMPL provides practical reporting guidance for statistical methods and statistical results so readers can understand, verify, and interpret quantitative findings clearly.

analysis.item_question: Are the statistical methods described in enough detail for a knowledgeable reader to understand and verify the analysis?
analysis.comment: The manuscript provides detailed equations (1-10) for all calculations, identifies specific data sources in Table 1, and describes the statistical approach for uncertainty and sensitivity analysis using Monte Carlo simulations.

guides.guideDescription: A synthesized reporting checklist for health and population modelling studies, extracted from Table 1 of Bennett and Manuel's review of existing guidance. The paper does not introduce a formal consensus guideline, but it compiles concrete reporting items across model structure, data, uncertainty, consistency, validity, implementation, transparency, and sponsorship for population modelling manuscripts.

analysis.item_question: Does the manuscript include a clear statement of the decision problem?
analysis.comment: The manuscript identifies the lack of quantification of P implications for SPC substitution as the core problem.
quotes: While land use, water use, and greenhouse gas benefits of dietary plant - based protein alternatives have been generally quantified, 15 the global and country - level P implications of replacing animal protein with SPC remain poorly defined .

analysis.item_name: State an objective consistent with the decision problem
analysis.item_question: Does the manuscript specify the objective of the evaluation, and is it consistent with the stated decision problem?
analysis.comment: The authors list three specific objectives aligned with quantifying P utilization, substitution potential, and circularity.
quotes: The specific objectives are to : 1) estimate current P utilization and footprint s for animal meat consumption in 177 countries... 2) quantify the country - level potential to substitute SPC... 3) evaluate the circularity potential of rP from SPC processing compared to P recycled via animal manure

analysis.item_question: Does the manuscript specify the primary decision-maker for whom the model is intended?
analysis.comment: The manuscript identifies policymakers as a target audience in the outlook section.
quotes: providing policymakers with additional considerations when generating methods to ensure security and resiliency in food production system s nationally and globally .

analysis.item_question: Does the manuscript clearly state the perspective of the model?
analysis.comment: The model takes a 'P footprint' perspective, defined as total fertilizer utilization for production.
quotes: T he term “P footprint” in this study refer s to the total amount of P fertilizer (i.e., synthetic and manure P ) utilized to generate a specific animal meat or plant - based protein commodity rather than P losses throughout later stages of food production

analysis.item_name: Make model inputs consistent with the perspective
analysis.item_question: Are the model inputs consistent with the stated perspective?

analysis.item_question: Are definitions of the variables in the model justified?

quotes: In this study, PUE is estimated as the P yield (i.e., P content) over inputs (i.e., P fertilizer and manure), where Zou et al. 32 calculated PUE for each individual crop in each country. This method of PUE calculation is the balance method

analysis.item_name: State and justify the scope of the model
analysis.item_question: Has the scope of the model been stated and justified?

analysis.item_name: Align outcomes with perspective, scope, and objective

analysis.comment: The outcomes (P reduction percentages and metric tons of P saved) directly address the objectives and resource perspective.

analysis.item_name: Use a model structure consistent with theory of the condition

quotes: The average P footprint for each country was estimated using an approach adapted and modified from Metson et al. 12 , shown in Equation s (1) - (3) .

analysis.item_name: Justify causal relationships in the structure

quotes: Equation (1) calculates the individual P footprint by country for meat consumption by combining the P contributions from each meat type consumed . Equation (2) calculates a P conversion factor for each animal type by combining the P contributions from all feed type s

analysis.item_question: Are differences in logic between baseline and alternative scenarios clearly described?
analysis.comment: The differences between the current baseline and the SPC substitution scenarios are clearly defined by the protein fraction limits and meat reduction hierarchy.
quotes: L imitation on the fraction of animal meat protein was evaluated from 0.5 to 0.0 kg animal meat protein per kg total protein. A ll animal meat protein in excess of the limit was substituted with SPC protein.

analysis.item_question: Are key algorithms, rules, or equations used in the model documented?
analysis.comment: The manuscript provides ten specific equations (Eq 1-10) documenting the logic for phosphorus footprinting, trade analysis, and recirculation calculations.
quotes: The average P footprint for each country was estimated using an approach adapted and modified from Metson et al. 12 , shown in Equation s (1) - (3) .

analysis.item_question: Are model components and their roles clearly described for the simulation paradigm used?
analysis.comment: The manuscript clearly defines the roles of various model components such as P use efficiency, feed conversion, and trade dynamics, with a summary provided in Table 1.
quotes: Table 1 . Sources for all parameters used in the methods for P footprint, SPC analysis, and P recirculation potential.

analysis.item_question: Are all data sources used for model development and experimentation described?
analysis.comment: The manuscript describes primary data sources including FAOSTAT and specific literature sources for various parameters, summarized in Table 1.
quotes: The Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) statistics database (FAOSTAT), 30 which includes country-level and trade data for primary and processed crops, plant and animal-based food use, and protein consumption was a primary data source for this analysis .
Table 1 includes sources and geospatial scale for all parameters used within the methods .

analysis.item_question: Are parameter values/distributions and their origins documented?
analysis.comment: Table 1 documents the origins of parameters, and the text provides specific values used for protein content, excretion rates, and recovery rates.
quotes: A protein content of 67.4% in SPC (dry-basis) 23 assuming 94% dry matter 40 was used to convert between SPC and protein generation ( Table S 5 ) .
The fraction of total P intake excreted in manure by animal type, P man_loss , used was 80% for bovine , 41 48% for goats/mutton , 42 50% for pigs, and 70% for poultry broilers .

analysis.item_question: Are data preprocessing and transformation steps reported?
analysis.comment: The manuscript details transformation steps using a series of equations (1-10) to convert raw agricultural and trade data into phosphorus footprint metrics.
quotes: Equation (2) calculates a P conversion factor for each animal type by combining the P contributions from all feed type s (i.e., roughages, food crops, and byproducts and concentrates) consumed by each animal type .
Equation (4) calculates the non - domestic fraction of each feed type based on the net imports and the domestic supply quantity of the feed.

analysis.item_question: Are explicit assumptions made during modelling and data preparation documented?
analysis.comment: The authors explicitly state assumptions regarding data years, PUE calculations for roughage, and conversion factors for soy protein.
quotes: Data was limited to 2019 to avoid potential effects of the COVID - 19 pandemic.
To still account for the P embedded in roughage and residues, a PUE of 1.0 was used such that P conversion factors , F , were based solely on the roughage and residue P content

analysis.item_question: Is model initialization clearly reported?
analysis.comment: The analysis is initialized using data from the 2019 FAOSTAT database and specific regional/country parameters provided in the text and supplementary tables.
quotes: All data sources were limited to open access databases for ease of reproducibility. T he Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) statistics database (FAOSTAT), 30... was a primary data source for this analysis . Data was limited to 2019 to avoid potential effects of the COVID - 19 pandemic.

analysis.item_question: Is run length and stopping condition clearly specified?
analysis.comment: The run length for the uncertainty analysis is explicitly stated as 10,000 Monte Carlo simulations. The substitution modeling evaluates a range from 0.5 to 0.0.
quotes: A total of 10,000 Monte Carlo simulations over the 177 countries were used to determine spearman’s rank coefficients of each uncertain parameter.
L imitation on the fraction of animal meat protein was evaluated from 0.5 to 0.0 kg animal meat protein per kg total protein.

analysis.item_question: Is the output estimation approach adequately described?
analysis.comment: The study describes the output estimation through ten formal equations and employs Spearman’s rank coefficients to characterize the sensitivity of results to parameter uncertainty.
quotes: A total of 10,000 Monte Carlo simulations over the 177 countries were used to determine spearman’s rank coefficients of each uncertain parameter. Spearman’s rank coefficients are included to demonstrate how sensitive results are to each parameter

analysis.item_question: Is the implementation software or programming language clearly identified?
analysis.comment: The manuscript describes the mathematical model and equations but does not identify the specific software, programming language, or platform used for the implementation and data analysis.

analysis.item_question: Are random-number generation and stochastic sampling procedures reported when relevant?
analysis.comment: The study reports performing 10,000 Monte Carlo simulations for uncertainty analysis, but it does not specify the random number generation (RNG) procedures, seeds, or the specific statistical distributions used for sampling parameters.
quotes: A total of 10,000 Monte Carlo simulations over the 177 countries were used to determine spearman’s rank coefficients of each uncertain parameter.

analysis.item_question: Is model execution control clearly described?
analysis.comment: The execution control of the substitution model is described, specifically the logic for reducing animal protein consumption in a specific order based on phosphorus conversion factors across a defined range.
quotes: Limitation on the fraction of animal meat protein was evaluated from 0.5 to 0.0 kg animal meat protein per kg total protein.

The substitution process was done by reducing the amount of animal - based protein by the meat type with the highest estimated P contributions (i.e., 1 bovine meat, 2 goat/mutton meat, 3 pigmeat, and 4 poultry meat).

analysis.item_question: Are system/hardware specifications and runtime context documented when relevant?
analysis.comment: The manuscript does not provide documentation regarding system hardware specifications or the runtime context for the simulations.

analysis.item_question: Is there a clear statement on model/code accessibility?
analysis.comment: While the study details the mathematical model and data sources, it does not include a statement regarding the accessibility of the code or scripts used to perform the calculations and simulations.

articleInfo.label: Synopsis
articleInfo.text:
Synopsis: This study analyzes the phosphorus benefits of substitution of animal meat protein with
soy protein across 177 countries.
quotes: Synopsis: This study analyzes
protein across 177 countries.

abstract.label: Abstract
abstract.text:
1
Animal meat protein consumption is typically associated with greater environmental impacts than
2
plant - based proteins . While most environmental impact assessments comparing animal and
3
plant - based proteins focus on water usage and carbon emissions, phosphorus (P) use is a key
4
parameter for a sustainable food system. C ountry - specific animal and crop parameters were
5
leveraged to evaluate the P footprint of current animal meat protein consumption and potential
6
benefits from substitution using soy - based protein. The global average P footprint for animal meat
7
protein consumption when considering synthetic and manure P fertilizers was an estimated 2.6
8
kg P y - 1 per capita in 2019 . A complete transition to soy protein concentrate (SPC) from animal
9
meat protein was estimated to decrease total P fertilizer applied to animal feed products by 81 % ,
10
or an estimated 8.3 million metric tons of P per year and 33% of the total estimated 2019 P
11
fertilizer utilization . Additionally, t he recovery of P during SPC processing could generate sufficient
12
renewable P fertilizer to replace an estimated 17% of total P utilized in global soybean production.
13
The reduction and efficient reuse of P from a transition to SPC as a primary protein source can
14
generate greater security and resiliency in global food systems.
15
16

introduction.label: Introduction
introduction.text:
Introduction
19
Phosphorus (P) is an essential crop nutrient critical to global food security. 1 Global P
20
consumption has increased nearly fivefold since 1961 due to mobilization of phosphate rock
21
reserves to support marked increases in crop productivity and human population , 2 but only 22%
22
of this P , 3 and similarly 23% of nitrogen used in food production, 4 is consumed directly by humans
23
in food products . Inefficient P usage can prematurely exhaust currently economically viable
24
phosphate rock reserves , 5 increase volatility in global fertilizer markets , 6 increase wastes
25
associated with P fertilizer production (e.g., phosphogypsum) , 7,8 and potentially lead to
26
eutrophication from losses to surface waters . 9 Because food production accounts for nearly 85%
27
of global P utilization , 10 diet ary choices directly shape global P demand .
28
The source of protein in human diets is a key determinant of dietary sustainability . D iets
29
containing a greater proportion of plant - based proteins generally supporting improved health
30
outcomes and lower environmental impacts due to more protective diet scores and potentially
31
lower greenhouse gas emissions, energy demand, and land use . 11 In terms of P footprints, initial
32
estimates indicate that animal - based products have a 8 - to 25 - times higher P footprint than plant -
33
based products from leguminous crops such as soybeans. 12,13 With global animal - based product
34
demand expected to increase nearly 38% 14 by 2050, a dietary shift towards plant - based protein s
35
could improve diet sustainability and food system resilienc e . 15,16 However, many desirable high
36
protein crops, including pulses, are not produced at at a rate to substitute animal proteins ,
37
meaning that large - scale adoption would require increases in production . 17
38
Soy protein foods have a long history as plant - based meat alternatives , 18 and soy
39
production exceeds total pulses production by over four times 19 . Soy protein concentrate (SPC) ,
40
derived from soybean meal , offers a promising high protein alternative as it is already used in
41
more recent plant - based meat alternatives 20 and provides high digestibility of amino acids with a
42
nearly complete amino acid profile comparable to animal - based protein s . 21 Currently, 98% of
43
https://doi.org/10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-9819w ORCID: https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4037-2939 Content not peer-reviewed by ChemRxiv. License: CC BY-NC 4.0

global soybean meal is fed to animals as a high protein feed , 22 indicating untapped potential to
44
divert existing SPC for direct human consumption without necessarily intensifying soybean
45
production . However, the potential for SPC to replace animal protein at the global and country
46
level has not been quantified . W hile land use, water use, and greenhouse gas benefits of dietary
47
plant - based protein alternatives have been generally quantified, 15 the global and country - level P
48
implications of replacing animal protein with SPC remain poorly defined . Moreover, because P
49
can be recovered as a renewable P (rP) fertilizer from wastewater generated during SPC
50
processing , 23 the circularity of P afforded by substitution to soybean protein has not been
51
accounted for in previous studies, underestimating the potential reduction in P footprint.
52
To fully evaluate the P reduction and reuse benefits of SPC substitution, country - level P
53
footprints and circularity must be individually determined. While previous studies have quantified
54
country - level P footprints for animal meat and plant - based commodities using global average P
55
conversion factors, 12 with few country - specific assessments, 24 – 28 recent country - level animal and
56
crop data (e.g., feed crop P use efficiency, feed conversion efficiency, animal systems, and
57
international trade) could be leveraged to improve these global estimates. Additionally, the
58
incorporation of trade of animal feedstocks in P footprint estimates can better capture embedded
59
P flows and circularity potential for animal meat and SPC consumption globally. 29
60
T his study aims to assess the potential of SPC from existing soy production and the
61
associated P benefits of substituting SPC for current animal - based protein s , including potential
62
reduction s in diet ary P burden and increased P circularity. The specific objectives are to : 1)
63
estimate current P utilization and footprint s for animal meat consumption in 177 countries, based
64
on annual synthetic and organic (i.e., manure) P fertilizer inputs , leveraging country - and region -
65
specific parameters and trade data ; 2) quantify the country - level potential to substitute SPC ,
66
produced from existing soybean meal, for animal meat protein (i.e., beef, goat/mutton, pig, and
67
poultry) and the associated P reduction benefits ; and 3) evaluate the circularity potential of rP
68
from SPC processing compared to P recycled via animal manure as a P fertilizer source .
69

Methods

Terminology and Data Sources
74
T he term “P footprint” in this study refer s to the total amount of P fertilizer (i.e., synthetic
75
and manure P ) utilized to generate a specific animal meat or plant - based protein commodity
76
https://doi.org/10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-9819w ORCID: https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4037-2939 Content not peer-reviewed by ChemRxiv. License: CC BY-NC 4.0

rather than P losses throughout later stages of food production ( Figure 1 ) . This is sometimes
77
referred to as the “virtual P” of a commodity in trade analyses, as opposed to the physical P , which
78
is the amount of P physically embedded in the commodity (i.e., P content). 29 All data sources were
79
limited to open access databases for ease of reproducibility. T he Food and Agriculture
80
Organization of the United Nations (FAO) statistics database (FAOSTAT), 30 which includes
81
country - level and trade data for primary and processed crops, plant and animal - based food use,
82
and protein consumption was a primary data source for this analysis . Data was limited to 2019 to
83
avoid potential effects of the COVID - 19 pandemic. A detailed table of regions used ( Table S1 )
84
and values for parameters ( Table S 2 - S 5 ) are provided in the Supplementary Information. Table
85
1 includes sources and geospatial scale for all parameters used within the methods .

Animal Meat Phosphorus Footprint

This section outlines the process for calculating the P footprint in each country for
92
consumption of cattle, goat / mutton, swine, and poultry meat . The P footprint represents the total
93
amount of phosphate rock - derived (e.g., synthetic fertilizer) and manure P utilized to produce the
94
feed crops consumed by these animals . S oil residual P was not considered in this analysis but
95
may be utilized to meet crop requirements , which may cause the total estimated fertilizer input to
96
be less than the total embedded P within a crop due to uptake of residual P. 36 Additionally, the
97
recirculation of P in manure was not considered in the P footprint, so manure P recirculated was
98
not subtracted from the overall P inputs and only total P input was considered instead of net P
99
input . The P footprint was calculated for 177 countries that had data available for fertilizer usage
100
and food consumption , allowing for comparison of how dietary changes can impact P needs . T he
101
average P footprint for each country was estimated using an approach adapted and modified from
102
Metson et al. 12 , shown in Equation s (1) - (3) . Equation (1) calculates the individual P footprint by
103
country for meat consumption by combining the P contributions from each meat type consumed .
104
Equation (2) calculates a P conversion factor for each animal type by combining the P
105

106
consumed by each animal type . Equation (3) calculates the P inputs for each animal feed type
107
consumed , factoring in both domestically produced and imported feeds.

Animal Roughage and Residue Feed
143
Though food crop and co - product/concentrate feeds could be captured by crop categories
144
considered in Zou et al. , 32 there were various roughage and residue feed types from Mottet et
145
al. 31 that could not be directly captured by these categories. However, roughages are a primary
146
feed for grazing ruminants in grasslands, and its inclusion is important to capturing the P utilization
147
for these ruminant animals. To still account for the P embedded in roughage and residues, a PUE
148
of 1.0 was used such that P conversion factors , F , were based solely on the roughage and residue
149
P content (i.e., kg P per kg roughage /residue ). T he P conversion factor was calculated for each
150
roughage/residue similarly to Equation (3) assuming all production was domestic (i.e., f d = 1 and
151
f nd = 0) . For categories that encompass ed multiple crop types (i.e., crop residues and pulse straw),
152
average F factors were generated for each country based on the proportion of each primary crop
153
produced as shown in Equation (6). Since crop residues were used as feed primarily in Asia and
154
Africa 31 (with minor usage for small ruminants in Europe), only the largest contributors to crop
155
residues were considered in those regions (i.e., maize, rice, wheat, and sugarcane) . 35

Soy Protein Analysis
161
To compare to current animal meat protein consumption , t he potential production of SPC
162
from current soybean meal generation was estimated . While soy protein isolate (SPI) is also
163
utilized in plant - based meat products, a majority is SPC. The production of SPC was based on
164
the acid leach procedure in which aqueous ethanol extraction is done . W ater is added to soybean
165
meal/defatted soy flour and the pH is adjusted to 4.5 and then centrifuged . T he supernatant is
166
removed and the residue is washed with water and centrifuged again produc ing SPC and a
167
second aqueous supernatant. 23 To determine the total SPC protein potential from soybean meal,
168
or “cake of soya beans” as reported by the FAO, a conversion rate of 20 kg SPC per 47.5 kg
169
soybean meal or 60 kg raw soybeans was assumed . 39 A protein content of 67.4% in SPC (dry -
170
basis) 23 assuming 94% dry matter 40 was used to convert between SPC and protein generation
171
( Table S 5 ) . A ll reported “cake of soya beans” from the FAO was also assumed to be capable of
172
be ing converted into defatted soybean flour.
173
T he substitution of SPC for animal meat protein was evaluated to determine effects on the
174
P utilization and P footprint. Developed countries tend to have the highest animal meat protein
175
consumption. 30 To equitably distribute animal meat reduction goals and to not unfairly burden
176
developing countries with low animal meat consumption limits , substitution was based on limiting
177
the fraction of protein per capita that can come from animal meat within a country (i.e., kg animal
178
protein per kg total protein) . This modeling scenario involves high meat - consuming countries
179
reducing their meat consumption prior to low meat - consuming countries. T he fraction of animal

Phosphorus Potential from Livestock Manure
196
Manure is the primary source of P recirculation potential in the meat production process.
197
For comparison of P re cycling potential between animal meat and soy - based protein production,
198

199

200

Phosphorus Recovery from Soy Protein Concentrate
208
Most soybeans are crushed to generate meal and to extract oil for usage in food and
209
biodiesel production. This approach provides centralized locations for soybean processing and
210
potential for P recovery. Laboratory analyses have shown the potential to recover P from further
211
processing of defatted soybean flour (i.e., finely ground soybean meal with oil extracted) into
212
SPC. 23 During production of SPC, aqueous waste streams (i.e., supernatant) are formed from
213
centrifugation that are rich in P , and require further treatment before discharge to the environment
214
at a water resource recovery facility . The P in these waste streams can be precipitated as calcium
215
phytate similarly to rP generation in corn ethanol biorefineries. 23 A general equation for rP
216
potential from SPC production was generated based on the amount of soybean meal processed
217
in Equation ( 8 ). 23

Embedded Phosphorus in Commodities
225

Analyzing the embedded P in both animal meat and SPC products can help elucidate the
226
potential upstream losses that occur from total P utilization as well as the P burden to water
227
resource recovery facilities. To determine the embedded P in animal meats, standard
228
concentrations of P based on live animal weight were used . The concentrations used for bovine,
229
goat/mutton, pig, and poultry meat were 6.6, 5.4, 5.0, and 5.8 kg P per MT meat, respectively. 44
230
For SPC, a concentration of 7.6 kg P per MT SPC was used . 45 To ensure that the embedded P
231
was not in excess of that remaining in the feed intake minus that in manure estimates, the
232
calculated embedded P was compared and was limited to that remaining when country and
233
animal level manure estimates were subtracted from the total feed intake (i.e., Remaining P =
234
Total Feed Intake P – Manure P).
235

discussion.label: Study Limitations
discussion.text:
Discussion
255
Animal Meat and Soy Phosphorus Utilization
256
The total global P utilization of fertilizers (i.e., synthetic and manure P) to produce animal
257
meat protein has increased an estimated 15% from 2014 to 2021 , with an estimated 19.8 million
258
metric tons ( Mt ) P used in 2019 ( Fig ure 2a ). The P utilization for poultry meat alone increased
259
nearly 37%, particularly in Asia and Africa, while bovine meat increased an estimated 17%. Brazil,
260
China, and the US utilized approximately 44% of all P associated with animal meat protein in
261
2019 . The P associated with bovine meat account ed for an estimated 55 to 58% of total global P
262
use for animal meat protein production , of which 78% was from embedded P in roughage feeds.
263
W hen considering only non - roughage feeds (i.e., food crops, co - products and concentrates) , the
264
total P utilization for animal meat was an estimated 10.5 Mt P per year in 2019 , 40 to 48% of
265
which was attributed to pigmeat feeds . Soybean meal was the largest estimated non - roughage
266
contributor to bovine and poultry P use ( Table S 6 ) .
267
The 2019 global average P footprint of fertilizer use for animal meat consumption was an
268
estimated 2.6 kg P per capita . The global P footprint of bovine meat was an estimated two to three
269
times larger than pig and poultry meat in 2019. Bovine meat was the largest estimated contributor
270
to P use in 80% of countries analyzed due to higher P conversion factors than other meats.
271
Country - level P footprint estimates are presented in Table S8. Countries in Sub - Saharan Africa
272
(SSA) had the lowest P footprints based on their country - level meat consumption , which was
273
lowest for Democratic Republic of the Congo and Mozambique at 0.1 and 0.3 kg P per capita per
274
year, respectively. These low P footprints were due to limited meat consumption , despite high P
275
https://doi.org/10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-9819w ORCID: https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4037-2939 Content not peer-reviewed by ChemRxiv. License: CC BY-NC 4.0

conversion factors. T he largest estimated P footprints from meat consumption were primarily in
276
Latin America and the Caribbean (LAC) at 10.0 (Brazil) and 8.6 (Argentina) kg P per capita per
277
year ( Fig ure 1 b ). These large footprints were primarily due to high meat consumption and P
278
conversion factors ( Fig ure S2 ) . A high P conversion factor was the result of poor feed conversion
279
efficiencies and elevated feed P us age due to below - average crop PUE , likely reflecting P - fix ation
280
and thus low fertilizer PUE in the generally weathered soils in Brazil . In particular, the LAC region
281
ruminant animals consum e a large r amount of fresh grass as part of their diet, which often leads
282
to lower feed conversion efficiencies due to greater consumption to meet nutrition and caloric
283
requirements. 31 Though grasslands are often seen as self - sustaining due to local cycling of
284
manure nutrients, the lack of supplemental P fertilizer and the transfer of manure to croplands
285
has led to an average negative P budget among global grasslands at - 1.5 kg P per hectare . 46
286
Above average feed crop PUEs and more efficient feed conversion for animal meat
287
proteins can greatly influence country - level animal meat protein P utilization estimates . T he
288
United States (US) had the highest meat consumption per capita of all countries in 2019 , but its
289
P footprint was estimated to be only 3.3 kg P per capita per year due to more efficient P conversion
290
factor s , consistent with a US - specific analysis that estimated 3.4 kg P per capita per year from
291
meat 27 . The use of high - energy feeds in the US, which have higher feed crop PUEs than grazing,
292
contributed to a lower P conversion factor and thus footprint . The consideration of country - specific
293
parameters, such as PUE and trade, had a large influence on individual P footprints : the US P
294
footprint for meat consumption was 23% lower than previous estimates that used a global average
295
P conversion factor . 12 Additionally, the incorporation of trade dynamics accounts for the improved
296
P productivity and complexity associated with feed trade, 47 where maintaining optimal trading is
297
a key component of global P cycling and sustainability. 48 The P footprint results were also
298
compared to country - specific studies, the approach in this study generally yielded higher P
299
footprint results due to the inclusion of manure P inputs , higher per capita meat consumption in
300
2019, and a resource - based method rather than emissions - based ( Table S7 ).
301
https://doi.org/10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-9819w ORCID: https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4037-2939 Content not peer-reviewed by ChemRxiv. License: CC BY-NC 4.0

A global sensitivity analysis was conducted to understand key drivers of global P utilization
302
for different animal meats. Sensitivity results generally revealed that ruminant animal and poultry
303
P utilization were most sensitive to changes in P content of feed, whereas pig P utilization had
304
greater sensitivity to the feed conversion efficiency, particularly in East and Southeast Asia
305
(ESEA) ( Fig ure S3 ). The sensitivity of ruminant animals to feed P content is likely due to relatively
306
large consumption of roughages, where roughage P contributions are based on an assumed PUE
307
of 1.0. All animal types exhibited moderate sensitivity to soybean meal P content as the dominant
308
feed type.
309
310
311

312
for production of meat consumed , including the contribution from roughages for bovine meat. (b) Country -
313
level total P footprint estimates (kg P utilized per capita per year) for 2019 bovine, goat, pig, and poultry
314
meat consumption. ( c ) Estimates of global protein potential for soy protein concentrate (SPC) production
315
from soybean meal and actual 2019 protein consumption of bovine, goat/mutton, pig, and poultry meats.
316
( d ) Estimated total P utilization to produce SPC and animal - based total protein , including fraction of P from
317
animal roughage feed .
318

Soy Protein Substitution Analysis
320
A transition from animal to plant - based proteins could dramatically reduce global P
321
consumption. The global average P utilization to produce a unit of animal - based protein was an
322
estimated 69 to 96% greater than what is required to generate an equivalent amount of SPC
323
protein from soybean meal. The potential production of SPC from current soybean meal
324
generation , estimated to be 62.3 Mt in 2019 , could displace all protein consumption from animal -
325
based meats (50.2 Mt in 2019, Figure 2c ). The conversion of all soybean meal into SPC rather
326
than animal feed would utilize an estimated 2.8 Mt P per year , approximately 14% of the total P
327
utilized to produce animal - based meat ( Figure 2d ). When considering only non - roughages,
328
production of SPC protein would still only require an estimated 30% of total P utilization of animal -
329
based protein.
330
Substitution of animal - based proteins with SPC could reduce non - roughage feed P
331
utilization by as much as 81 % , or 33% of all P inputs in 2019 . The largest estimated P utilization
332
decreases were in the US, China, and Brazil. As complete substitution is u n likely, replacement
333
was evaluated by limiting country - level fraction s of total protein consumption from animal sources
334
(i.e., animal meat protein fraction) and substituting meat protein with SPC . When limiting the
335
fraction of protein consumption from animals , s ubstitution would start in the high meat consuming
336
regions of NA, LAC, and Oceania (OCE) followed by WE, Eastern Europe ( EE ) , and ESEA and
337
finally North East and North Africa (NENA) , SA, and SSA ( Fig ure S 4 ). The hypothetical
338
subs titution of animal meat protein with SPC was done by reducing meat protein in succession
339
based on highest overall P conversion factors (i.e., 1 bovine meat, 2 goat/mutton meat, 3 pigmeat,
340
and 4 poultry meat) . However, it is likely that the global structure and system of livestock
341
production will change in the future due to climate change and demographic shifts , which could
342
impact the priority reductions. With limited information on dietary and livestock trends, data was
343
restricted to 2019. The change in P footprint for each animal meat by country based on SPC
344
substitution is shown in Table S8.
345
https://doi.org/10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-9819w ORCID: https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4037-2939 Content not peer-reviewed by ChemRxiv. License: CC BY-NC 4.0

Human consumption of SPC from country - level soybean meal production could reduce
346
global P utilization by an estimated 12.7 Mt P per year , or a 64% reduction . Comparatively, a 50%
347
substitution of animal products with plant - based meat and milk alternatives by 2050 could nearly
348
halt the net reduction in forest and natural land s and decrease agriculture and land use
349
greenhouse gas emissions by 31%. 15 Substitution with SPC resulted in a consistent global decline
350
in total P utilization across animal - based protein limits , with a more rapid decline in roughage -
351
based P due to initial replacement of ruminant meats ( Fig ure 3a ). The bovine meat P footprint
352
had the largest country - level reductions up until an animal meat protein fraction of 0.2 (i.e., kg
353
animal to kg total protein) , at which point P footprint reductions occurred across all meat types. P
354
utilization for non - roughage feed types (e.g., crops, co - product residues) exhibited a steeper
355
decline starting at an animal meat protein limit of 0.2 , with a moderate decline between 0.5 and
356
0.2 . This trend was due to countries with lower animal meat protein consumption favoring
357
monogastric consumption , which primarily consume non - roughages, over ruminant consumption .
358
Additionally, there was only a minimal decline between 0.5 and 0.4 due to the small number of
359
countries that consume greater than 0.4 kg animal protein per kg total protein.
360
Developed countries can benefit the most from SPC replacement due to their high meat
361
consumption and associated P utilization. However, the P burden from developing countries with
362
increasing meat consumption may outstrip these benefits, particularly considering developing
363
countries had less efficient P conversion factors for animal meat proteins leading to higher P
364
utilization. The largest overall P benefits could be realized by replacing ruminant proteins due to
365
high ruminant P intakes from roughage feeds and relatively inefficient feed conversion. However ,
366
when roughage feeds are not considered, monogastric animals had the highest P utilization due
367
to their consumption of feed crops and co - products that are the most highly P fertilized.
368
Replacement of monogastric with plant - based protein could lead to greater decreases in synthetic
369
P fertilizer usage, as has been f ound for nitrogen (N) 49 . Soybean meal was estimated to be the
370
https://doi.org/10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-9819w ORCID: https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4037-2939 Content not peer-reviewed by ChemRxiv. License: CC BY-NC 4.0

largest contributor to P utilization of non - roughages, where direct consumption as SPC could
371
avoid the inefficiencies of protein production associated with animal production.
372
As most soybean meal is fed to animals within the country of cultivation , t he current global
373
distribution of soybean meal production could be used to substitute an estimated 73% of total
374
local animal meat protein consumption locally with SPC . The most populous countries including
375
China, India, and the US produce sufficient soybean meal to potentially substitute all animal meat
376
protein consumption (20.5 Mt protein per year) with soy - based protein ( Fig ure 3 b ) , reducing the
377
P utilization in these countries by 6.3 M t P per year of both synthetic and organic fertilizer . To fully
378
substitute SPC for animal - based proteins in countries deficient in soybean meal, an estimated
379
12.5 Mt SPC protein per year would need to be redistributed from countries where most soybean
380
meal is produced , resulting in an additional 5 Mt P per year reduction ( Fig ure 3 c ).
381
https://doi.org/10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-9819w ORCID: https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4037-2939 Content not peer-reviewed by ChemRxiv. License: CC BY-NC 4.0

382
Fig ure 3 . Soy protein substitution analysis. (a) Estimates of the 2019 P utilization for modeled SPC and
383
animal - based protein consumption under varying limits of animal meat protein consumption for each
384
country considering replacement in order of highest/priority P conversion factor (i.e., bovine meat,
385
goat/mutton meat, pigmeat, and poultry meat ) . ( b ) Estimates of the ratio between current SPC protein
386
potential from domestically produced soybean meal and animal - based protein consumption . ( c ) Estimated
387
transfers of SPC protein necessary between major regions to fully substitute animal - based protein
388
https://doi.org/10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-9819w ORCID: https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4037-2939 Content not peer-reviewed by ChemRxiv. License: CC BY-NC 4.0

consumption . Regions include East and Southeast Asia (ESEA), Oceania (OCE), Latin America and the
389
Caribbean (LAC), North America (NA), South Asia (SA), Eastern Europe (EE), Western Europe (WE), Near
390
East and North Africa (NENA), and Sub - Saharan Africa (SSA).
391
392
Phosphorus Recirculation Analysis
393
The P footprint of animal meat can be reduced through the reuse of manure nutrients to
394
grow feed . When considering the recirculation potential of P during animal meat production, an
395
estimated 61% (12 Mt P per year) of total global P utilization for animal feeds was embedded in
396
animal wastes that can be recirculated. During SPC processing, rP can also be captured and
397
reused as a renewable P fertilizer in soybean cultivation. R ecovery of r P during SPC processing
398
through chemical separation and reuse as a renewable fertilizer could replace an estimated 17%
399
of P fertilizer (0.34 Mt rP per year) used in soybean production globally ( Fig ure 4a ) , with the
400
potential for higher replacement in more agriculturally dense regions such as the Corn Belt of the
401
US . U nlike manure, the production of rP from SPC processing can be more easily increased
402
through process design and phytase incorporation, which can further enhance its circularity
403
potential. 50,51 While manure produced during animal meat production has greater P reuse
404
potential , m anure is not a balanced fertilizer that can be easily applied based on specific crop
405
needs due to inconsistent nutrient composition between animals and an N:P ratio that may not
406
match crop needs. 52 Animal manures generally have lower N:P than crop N:P needs , meaning
407
that application of manure to meet crop N requirements will lead to overapplication of P 53 and
408
increase P loss risk to surface waters. 54 Additional barriers including cost, land availability, and
409
transport logistics often lead to manure being treated as a waste material rather than a useful
410
fertilizer and not being strategically applied based on nutrient needs. 55 Alternatively, applying
411
manure on a P basis for crop production would maximize P utilization, and for non - legume crops
412
would co - offset a substantial portion of N fertilizer needs, the remainder of which could be met by
413
supplementary N fertilization. This pollution burden is also seen in the larger greywater
414
https://doi.org/10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-9819w ORCID: https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4037-2939 Content not peer-reviewed by ChemRxiv. License: CC BY-NC 4.0

requirements of animal products to assimilate added pollution than oil crops such as soybeans. 56
415
Whereas the P recovered during SPC processing can be applied more efficiently based on crop
416
requirements and easily exported to nutrient constrained areas .
417
While P can be recovered in animal wastes and SPC as rP , a large fraction of P is
418
embedded in the protein product s. The e mbedded P in animal meat protein and SPC constitutes
419
an estimated 9% (1.8 Mt P per year) and 28% (0.5 Mt P per year) of the ir total P utilization ,
420
respectively . This P is eventually either excreted in waste streams or absorbed by the consumer.
421
Some evidence has suggested that plant - based diets could elevate the P loading to wastewater
422
infrastructure , due to the transition to more P - dense beans and legumes as a protein source . 57
423
However, one potential benefit is that P wastes become further centralized and can be further
424
recovered at wastewater facilities , rather than lost during other processing steps where it cannot
425
be recovered.
426
The amount of P lost in processing that is neither embedded in the protein product nor
427
recovered from waste was estimated to be five times higher for animal meat protein (5.9 Mt P per
428
year) than for SPC (1.1 Mt P per year) assuming usage of all potential recycled P (i.e., animal
429
waste or rP) . On a country - level, the per - capita processing P loss potential was on average an
430
estimated 5.8 times higher for animal than soy - based protein consumption. Countries in the LAC,
431
WE, EE, and ESEA regions in particular had high processing P losses (up to 3.7 kg P per capita
432
per year) from animal meat protein consumption even when assuming complete recirculation of
433
waste P ( Fig ure 4b ) due to high P utilization for animal feed crops in those regions from below -
434
average PUEs . The consumption of SPC protein reduced processing P loss potential across most
435
countries, with Brazil having the highest loss potential at an estimated 0.8 kg P per capita per
436
year ( Fig ure 4c ). The lower processing P loss from SPC substitution is primarily due to reduced
437
P utilization in SPC despite a smaller overall P reuse potential, resulting from better PUEs for soy
438
production and a lower P conversion factor. However, some high meat consuming countries,
439
including the US, have relatively low processing P losses and footprints from animal proteins
440
https://doi.org/10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-9819w ORCID: https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4037-2939 Content not peer-reviewed by ChemRxiv. License: CC BY-NC 4.0

when compared to SPC due to greater feeding efficiencies at concentrated animal feeding
441
operations (CAFOs). However , the continued intensification of these operations has exacerbated
442
geospatial concentrat ion of manure and P loadings and non - point source pollution . 58 The
443
s ubstitution of animal protein with SPC even in more P efficient countries would help to reduce
444
the burden of CAFOs on water bodies, including the overapplication of P from CAFO manures.
445
446
https://doi.org/10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-9819w ORCID: https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4037-2939 Content not peer-reviewed by ChemRxiv. License: CC BY-NC 4.0

Fig ure 4 . Phosphorus recirculation potential. (a) Estimates of the 2019 total P utilization , P recirculation
447
from animal waste and rP , and embedded P for animal - based protein and SPC, respectively. Estimates of
448
the footprint of processing P loss (i.e., P Utilization – Embedded P – Manure P/rP) for ( b ) animal meat
449
proteins and ( c ) SPC .
450
451
Study Limitations
452
This study provides a novel approach to estimating P utilization and savings based on the
453
type of protein consumed at a country - level, accounting for country - specific parameters and trade
454
dynamics into estimates. However, the global scale of this study necessitat es several simplifying
455
assumptions were made when generating P estimates. Though many parameters can vary
456
among individual livestock species across varying geographical scales ( e.g. , feed conversion
457
efficiency, feed ration composition , excretion rates ) , regional and country - based parameters were
458
used when generating P utilization and recirculation estimates for animal meat protein leading to
459
certain countries appearing more or less P efficient based on the other countries in the region in
460
which they were grouped. Country and intraregional - level values for feed conversion efficiency
461
and feed fraction could improve accuracy of estimates. Another consideration is the change in
462
PUE and livestock composition over time, as this study used 2019 data in its estimates. Though
463
the PUE was based on country - level estimates, PUE often changes as a country develops where
464
it declines during early development, improves during sustainable intensification, and eventually
465
levels off post - development. 32 The methodology for calculating PUE can also influence P
466
estimates, with the balance method used in this study leading likely to overestimates of the total
467
P usage. This leads to limitations in using a fixed year for PUE estimates, as it may improve or
468
decline over time depending on the stage of development of a country. Additionally, the approach
469
used to calculate PUE can be more refined as current approaches often have discrepancies
470
between calculated PUE. 38 The composition of livestock would also change over time

abstract.sample: 1 Animal meat protein consumption is typically associated with greater environmental impacts than 2 plant - ba ... generate greater security and resiliency in global food systems. 15 16 Graphical Abstract:
abstract.countedText:
1
Animal meat protein consumption is typically associated with greater environmental impacts than
2
plant - based proteins . While most environmental impact assessments comparing animal and
3
plant - based proteins focus on water usage and carbon emissions, phosphorus (P) use is a key
4
parameter for a sustainable food system. C ountry - specific animal and crop parameters were
5
leveraged to evaluate the P footprint of current animal meat protein consumption and potential
6
benefits from substitution using soy - based protein. The global average P footprint for animal meat
7
protein consumption when considering synthetic and manure P fertilizers was an estimated 2.6
8
kg P y - 1 per capita in 2019 . A complete transition to soy protein concentrate (SPC) from animal
9
meat protein was estimated to decrease total P fertilizer applied to animal feed products by 81 % ,
10
or an estimated 8.3 million metric tons of P per year and 33% of the total estimated 2019 P
11
fertilizer utilization . Additionally, t he recovery of P during SPC processing could generate sufficient
12
renewable P fertilizer to replace an estimated 17% of total P utilized in global soybean production.
13
The reduction and efficient reuse of P from a transition to SPC as a primary protein source can
14
generate greater security and resiliency in global food systems.
15
16
